title: French Vocabulary and Grammar Basics for Beginners
goal: Learn French vocabulary and grammar basics
metaDescription: French Vocabulary and Grammar Basics for Beginners — a free beginner-level guide covering learn french vocabulary and grammar basics. Learn with clear...

roadmap.title: French Vocabulary and Grammar Basics for Beginners

modules.title: Foundations and Pronunciation
modules.focus: Master the French alphabet, accent marks, and fundamental pronunciation rules to read and speak basic words correctly.
objectives: Identify French letters and their corresponding phonetic sounds
Pronounce distinct French sounds such as nasal vowels and the guttural 'R'
Recognize and name the five diacritical accent marks and their functions
Distinguish between silent and pronounced consonants at the end of words
modules.description: Master the French alphabet, accent marks, and fundamental pronunciation rules to read and speak basic words correctly.

modules.title: Greetings and Subject Pronouns
modules.focus: Learn essential greeting vocabulary and the subject pronouns alongside the irregular verb 'être' (to be).
objectives: Use appropriate greetings and farewells for different times of day and formality levels
Memorize the French subject pronouns (je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles)
Conjugate the irregular verb 'être' in the present tense
Introduce yourself and state basic personal information using 'être'
modules.description: Learn essential greeting vocabulary and the subject pronouns alongside the irregular verb 'être' (to be).

modules.title: Nouns, Gender, and Articles
modules.focus: Understand the concept of grammatical gender in French nouns and how to use definite and indefinite articles.
objectives: Determine whether a noun is masculine or feminine
Match indefinite articles (un, une, des) to nouns based on gender and number
Match definite articles (le, la, l', les) to nouns based on gender and number
Form plural nouns by applying standard spelling rules
modules.description: Understand the concept of grammatical gender in French nouns and how to use definite and indefinite articles.

modules.title: Numbers, Dates, and Time
modules.focus: Learn French numbers, days of the week, months, and how to tell time.
objectives: Count from 0 to 100 using correct French number combinations
State the days of the week, months of the year, and how to say the date
Ask and answer the question 'Quelle heure est-il ?' to tell time
Use related vocabulary such as 'matin', 'après-midi', and 'soir'
modules.description: Learn French numbers, days of the week, months, and how to tell time.

modules.title: Regular Verbs in the Present Tense
modules.focus: Learn to conjugate and use regular -er, -ir, and -re verbs in the present tense.
objectives: Identify the infinitive form of a verb and its corresponding ending group
Conjugate regular -er verbs like 'parler' in the present tense
Conjugate regular -ir verbs like 'finir' in the present tense
Conjugate regular -re verbs like 'vendre' in the present tense
Build simple affirmative sentences using subject pronouns and regular verbs
modules.description: Learn to conjugate and use regular -er, -ir, and -re verbs in the present tense.

modules.title: Essential Irregular Verbs
modules.focus: Master the conjugation and usage of the three most common irregular verbs: 'avoir' (to have), 'aller' (to go), and 'faire' (to do/make).
objectives: Conjugate 'avoir' in the present tense and use it to express age
Conjugate 'aller' in the present tense
Conjugate 'faire' in the present tense
Use these verbs in common expressions like 'faire du sport' or 'avoir besoin de'
modules.description: Master the conjugation and usage of the three most common irregular verbs: 'avoir' (to have), 'aller' (to go), and 'faire' (to do/make).

modules.title: Negative Sentences and Basic Questions
modules.focus: Learn how to make sentences negative and formulate basic yes/no questions.
objectives: Wrap verbs with 'ne...pas' to create negative sentences
Apply other common negative structures such as 'ne...jamais' (never)
Form questions using rising intonation
Form questions using the 'Est-ce que...' framework
modules.description: Learn how to make sentences negative and formulate basic yes/no questions.

modules.title: Adjectives and Descriptive Agreement
modules.focus: Learn vocabulary for common adjectives and the rules for making them agree with the nouns they modify in gender and number.
objectives: Make regular adjectives feminine by adding an 'e'
Make adjectives plural by adding an 's'
Place adjectives correctly in a sentence, recognizing that most follow the noun but BANGS adjectives precede it
Describe physical traits and personality using a vocabulary list of 20 common adjectives
modules.description: Learn vocabulary for common adjectives and the rules for making them agree with the nouns they modify in gender and number.

modules.title: Question Words and Information Gathering
modules.focus: Learn interrogative vocabulary to ask for specific information beyond yes/no questions.
objectives: Ask questions using 'qui' (who), 'que/quoi' (what), 'où' (where), 'quand' (when), 'pourquoi' (why), and 'comment' (how)
Use the interrogative adjective 'quel' and make it agree with the noun
Construct information questions using 'est-ce que' and question words
Formulate questions using basic inversion for present tense verbs
modules.description: Learn interrogative vocabulary to ask for specific information beyond yes/no questions.

modules.title: Possessive and Demonstrative Adjectives
modules.focus: Learn to express ownership and point out specific items using possessive and demonstrative adjectives.
objectives: Use possessive adjectives (mon, ton, son, etc.) correctly, noting that they agree with the possessed object's gender and number
Use demonstrative adjectives (ce, cette, ces) to point out specific nouns
Distinguish between the masculine 'ce' and the special 'cet' used before vowel sounds
Combine possessives and demonstratives with previously learned vocabulary to describe belongings
modules.description: Learn to express ownership and point out specific items using possessive and demonstrative adjectives.

modules.title: Prepositions of Location and Movement
modules.focus: Learn vocabulary for common places and the prepositions used to express location and movement.
objectives: Memorize vocabulary for 15 common places in a city (e.g., la boulangerie, l'école)
Use the preposition 'à' to express being at or going to a specific place
Use the preposition 'de' to express coming from a specific place
Use prepositions like 'dans', 'sur', and 'sous' to describe where objects are located
modules.description: Learn vocabulary for common places and the prepositions used to express location and movement.

modules.title: The Near Future and Immediate Past
modules.focus: Learn to express upcoming events and things that just happened using compound structures with 'aller' and 'venir de'.
objectives: Construct the near future tense using 'aller' in the present tense plus an infinitive verb
Construct the immediate past tense using 'venir de' in the present tense plus an infinitive verb
Create sentences describing what you are going to do tomorrow
Create sentences describing what you just did today
modules.description: Learn to express upcoming events and things that just happened using compound structures with 'aller' and 'venir de'.

modules.title: Foundations and Pronunciation
modules.content:
## The French Writing System and the IPA

Imagine you are handed a menu in a Parisian café. You see the word *eau*. It is only three letters long, yet it is pronounced simply as "o." A few lines down, you see *œufs*—five letters crammed together that collapse into a single, clipped sound. French spelling can feel like a secret code, but there is a method to the madness. 

French uses the same 26 letters as the English alphabet. However, the way those letters combine to create sounds is vastly different. English pronunciation is notoriously unpredictable; the letter combination "ough" can sound completely different in *though*, *through*, *tough*, and *cough*. French, on the other hand, is highly regulated. Once you learn the rules, you can accurately pronounce almost any word you read, even if you don't know its meaning.

To master these rules, language learners and linguists use a tool called the **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)**. The IPA is a standardized system where every single sound in human speech has its own unique symbol. For example, the sound of the letter "o" in *eau* is represented in the IPA as **[o]**. Throughout this chapter, IPA symbols will appear in square brackets to help you understand exactly how a word should sound, removing the guesswork from French pronunciation.

## The French Alphabet

The French alphabet contains the exact same 26 letters as the English alphabet. The primary difference lies in the names of the letters. When spelling out a word—such as giving your name over the phone—you will need to know the French letter names.

Here are the French names of the alphabet, with their approximate English sounds:

*   **A** - ah
*   **B** - bay
*   **C** - say
*   **D** - day
*   **E** - uh
*   **F** - eff
*   **G** - zhay
*   **H** - ash
*   **I** - ee
*   **J** - zhee
*   **K** - ka
*   **L** - ell
*   **M** - emm
*   **N** - enn
*   **O** - oh
*   **P** - pay
*   **Q** - kew
*   **R** - air
*   **S** - ess
*   **T** - tay
*   **U** - ew (said with lips tightly pursed)
*   **V** - vay
*   **W** - dooblvay
*   **X** - eeks
*   **Y** - ee-grek
*   **Z** - zed

### The Curious Case of 'H'

In French, the letter **H** is completely silent. It makes no sound whatsoever. 

*   The word *hiver* (winter) is pronounced exactly like *iver* [i-vair].
*   The word *homme* (man) is pronounced exactly like *omme* [om].

You simply skip over the H when reading a word. 

### Vowels vs. Consonants

Before diving into specific sounds, it helps to define these two terms. **Vowels** are sounds made with an open vocal tract; the air flows freely out of the mouth without being blocked by the tongue or lips (A, E, I, O, U). **Consonants** are sounds where the airflow is partially or fully blocked (B, C, D, F, etc.). 

French vowels are generally much tenser than English vowels. When speaking English, your lips and tongue are relatively relaxed. When speaking French, you must deliberately shape your mouth and purse your lips for vowel sounds.

## The Guttural 'R'

Perhaps the most iconic sound in the French language is the **guttural 'R'**. English speakers produce their "R" by curling the tongue back in the mouth. The French "R" is produced in a completely different part of the mouth.

To pronounce the French R:
1. Keep your tongue flat and relaxed at the bottom of your mouth.
2. Raise the very back of your tongue toward the soft palate (the squishy area at the roof of the back of your mouth).
3. Push air out, allowing the back of your tongue to lightly scrape or vibrate against the soft palate.

It should sound like a gentle gargle or a soft purr. It is not the harsh, hacking sound of a Spanish "J", nor is it a hard English "R". 

**Practice words for the French R:**
*   *Paris* [pa-ree]
*   *Bonjour* [bon-zhoor]
*   *Rouge* [roozh] (red)

## Nasal Vowels

Another hallmark of French pronunciation is the **nasal vowel**. In English, we lower our soft palate when we say "M," "N," or "NG," allowing air to escape through the nose. In French, vowels themselves can be nasalized. 

To make a nasal vowel, you pronounce the vowel sound while simultaneously lowering your soft palate, allowing air to flow out of both your mouth and your nose. 

There are three primary nasal vowels you will encounter as a beginner:

1.  **[ɑ̃]** - Similar to the "ah" in "father," but pushed through the nose. Found when 'a' or 'e' is followed by 'n' or 'm'. (e.g., *enfant* [ahn-fahn] - child).
2.  **[ɛ̃]** - Similar to the "e" in "bed," but nasalized. Found when 'i', 'e', or 'ai' is followed by 'n' or 'm'. (e.g., *matin* [mah-tan] - morning, *pain* [pan] - bread).
3.  **[ɔ̃]** - Similar to the "o" in "song," but nasalized. Found when 'o' or 'u' is followed by 'n' or 'm'. (e.g., *bon* [bon] - good, *nom* [non] - name).

**The Golden Rule of Nasal Vowels:** The consonant that creates the nasal sound (N or M) is **not pronounced**. You only use it to nasalize the vowel preceding it. Once you hit the nasal sound, the N or M vanishes. *Enfant* is not pronounced "en-fant"; it is "ahn-fahn".

### When Nasal Vowels Break

If a word has a double 'n' or double 'm', or if a vowel immediately follows the 'n' or 'm', the nasalization is broken. The vowel is pronounced normally, and the N or M is pronounced as a hard consonant.

*   *Bon* [bon] (good) - Nasal
*   *Bonne* [bon] (good, feminine) - Not nasal. The 'e' breaks the nasal sound, so the 'n' is pronounced.
*   *Pain* [pan] (bread) - Nasal
*   *Penne* [pen] (pasta) - Not nasal.

## The Letter 'E': The Silent Workhorse

The letter **E** behaves uniquely in French. It is the only letter that can be entirely silent at the end of a word, and its pronunciation changes depending on its placement.

1.  **The E caduc (The dropped E):** When 'E' appears at the end of a word (and is the only vowel), it is usually silent. The word *le* (the) is pronounced simply as [luh]—and in fast, everyday speech, that 'e' often vanishes entirely, sounding just like [l]. The word *je* (I) sounds like [zhuh], but often drops to [zh] before a vowel.
2.  **The É and È:** As we will see in the accents section, adding an accent to an 'E' forces it to be pronounced clearly.
3.  **The 'EU' combination:** When 'E' and 'U' combine, they create a sound that does not exist in English: **[ø]**. To make this sound, say the English letter "E" (ee), but forcefully round your lips as if you are blowing out a candle. (e.g., *deux* [duh] - two, *feu* [fuh] - fire).

## Diacritical Accent Marks

French uses five **diacritical accent marks**—small symbols added to letters to change their pronunciation or meaning. These are not optional decorations; they are mandatory parts of the spelling. 

### 1. The Acute Accent (L'accent aigu)
*   **Symbol:** **é**
*   **Function:** This accent only ever appears on the letter 'E'. It tells you to pronounce a sharp, clear "ay" sound **[e]**. Without the accent, a final 'e' is silent. With the accent, it must be pronounced.
*   **Example:** *Été* [ay-tay] (summer). Notice how both E's have the accent, and both are pronounced. 

### 2. The Grave Accent (L'accent grave)
*   **Symbol:** **è**
*   **Function:** This accent appears on E, A, and U. On the 'E', it dictates an open "eh" sound **[ɛ]**, like the 'e' in "bed." On 'A' and 'U', it does not change the pronunciation at all; it is used strictly to distinguish between words that are otherwise spelled identically.
*   **Examples:** 
    *   *Mère* [mair] (mother) - changes the E sound.
    *   *À* [ah] (at/to) - distinguishes the preposition from the verb *a* (has).
    *   *Où* [oo] (where) - distinguishes the question word from *ou* (or).

### 3. The Circumflex (L'accent circonflexe)
*   **Symbol:** **ê, â, î, ô, û**
*   **Function:** This little hat can go over any vowel. Historically, it represents an "S" that existed in older French (and Latin) but was dropped over time. For example, *hôtel* used to be *hostel*, and *île* used to be *isle*. Today, it generally lengthens the vowel sound slightly, though the difference is subtle. On 'I' and 'U', it serves no phonetic purpose and is mostly there for historical spelling reasons.
*   **Example:** *Forêt* [foh-reh] (forest), *Château* [shah-toh] (castle).

### 4. The Diaeresis (Le tréma)
*   **Symbol:** **ë, ï, ü**
*   **Function:** The diaeresis indicates that two vowels sitting next to each other must be pronounced separately, rather than blending into a single sound. It breaks up a diphthong.
*   **Example:** *Noël* [no-el] (Christmas). Without the tréma, "oe" might blend together. The tréma forces you to pronounce the 'O', then the 'E' separately. Another example is *maïs* [mah-ees] (corn), which is distinct from *mais* [meh] (but).

### 5. The Cedilla (La cédille)
*   **Symbol:** **ç**
*   **Function:** The cedilla appears only under the letter 'C'. Normally, a 'C' is pronounced as a hard "K" sound before A, O, and U (like *cat* or *cool*), and as a soft "S" sound before E and I (like *center*). The cedilla forces the 'C' to take the soft "S" sound, even when it is followed by an A, O, or U.
*   **Example:** *Garçon* [gar-son] (boy). Without the cedille, the 'C' before the 'O' would sound like a "K" (*garkon*). The cedille ensures it sounds like an "S" (*garson*).

## Consonants at the End of Words: The C-R-F-L Rule

In English, we almost always pronounce the consonants at the ends of words. *Cat* ends with a hard "T." *Dog* ends with a hard "G." 

In French, the default rule is the exact opposite: **consonants at the end of a word are silent.**

*   *Mot* (word) is pronounced [mo]. The T is silent.
*   *Paris* (Paris) is pronounced [pa-ree]. The S is silent.
*   *Frappe* (hit) is pronounced [frap]. The E is silent, and the P is pronounced because it is not the final letter.

This rule simplifies reading immensely. If you see a word ending in a consonant, you generally just ignore that final letter. 

### The C-R-F-L Exceptions

If the default is silence, what are the exceptions? There is a famous mnemonic device in French learning called the **C-R-F-L rule**. If a word ends in **C, R, F,** or **L** (think of the word *CaReFuL*), the consonant is usually pronounced.

*   **C:** *Avec* [ah-vek] (with), *Sac* [sak] (bag)
*   **R:** *Bonjour* [bon-zhoor] (hello), *Hiver* [ee-vair] (winter)
*   **F:** *Neuf* [nuf] (nine), *Chef* [shef] (boss/chef)
*   **L:** *Travel* [tra-vayl] (work), *Festival* [fes-tee-val] (festival)

**Important Caveat:** The C-R-F-L rule is a helpful guideline, but it is not absolute. There are exceptions. For example, *est* (is) ends in T, but the T is silent [eh]. *Fils* (son) ends in S, but the S is silent [feess]. Conversely, *sous* (under) ends in S, but the S is pronounced [soos]. When in doubt, consult a dictionary that includes IPA transcriptions.

### The "Liaison" Phenomenon

Why does French have so many silent letters if they just get ignored? The answer lies in a concept called **liaison**. 

Because French values a smooth, unbroken flow of sound (often called a "legato" speaking style), those silent consonants are not useless. When a word ending in a silent consonant is immediately followed by a word beginning with a vowel or a silent 'H', the silent consonant suddenly "wakes up" and links the two words together.

**A Concrete Scenario:**
Look at the word *vous* (you). On its own, it is pronounced [voo]. The 'S' is completely silent.
Now look at the word *avez* (have). It is pronounced [ah-vay].
If you say "you have" (*vous avez*), pronouncing them separately would sound choppy: [voo] [ah-vay]. 
Instead, French uses liaison. The silent 'S' in *vous* wakes up, turns into a 'Z' sound, and bridges the gap: [voo-zah-vay]. 

Liaisons only happen from the end of one word to the beginning of the next. They are the reason French sounds so fluid and connected. While you do not need to memorize every liaison rule right now, recognizing that silent consonants exist to facilitate these connections will help you understand why French spelling retains letters that are otherwise unpronounced.

## Key Takeaways

*   French uses the same 26 letters as English, but the letter **H** is always silent.
*   The **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)** provides a standardized symbol for every sound, removing the guesswork from pronunciation.
*   The **guttural 'R'** is produced at the back of the mouth by vibrating the soft palate, not by curling the tongue.
*   **Nasal vowels** are produced by pushing air through both the nose and mouth. The N or M that creates the nasal sound is never pronounced itself.
*   French has five **accent marks**: the acute (é), grave (è, à, ù), circumflex (ê, â, î, ô, û), diaeresis (ë, ï, ü), and cedilla (ç). Each alters pronunciation or distinguishes between identical words.
*   The default rule for final consonants is **silence**. However, words ending in **C, R, F, or L** (the *CaReFuL* rule) generally pronounce those final letters.
*   Silent consonants often wake up to form a **liaison** when the following word begins with a vowel, creating the smooth, connected flow characteristic of spoken French.

modules.title: Greetings and Subject Pronouns
modules.content:
## Bonjour! Navigating French Greetings

Imagine you are walking into a small boulangerie (bakery) in Paris on a Tuesday morning. The shop is warm, the air smells like fresh croissants, and the baker looks up as you enter. In French culture, entering a shop without greeting the shopkeeper is considered rude. You cannot simply point at a baguette and hand over your money. You must acknowledge them first. 

But which word do you use? The answer depends entirely on two things: the time of day, and your relationship with the person you are speaking to. French has distinct greetings for the morning, the afternoon, and the evening, and an entirely separate system for speaking to friends versus strangers.

### Greetings by Time of Day

The most common French greeting is **Bonjour**. Literally translated, it means "good day." 

*   **Bonjour** (Good morning / Good day): Used from the morning until the early evening. It is the safest, most universal greeting in the French language. If you are unsure what to say, say *bonjour*. 
*   **Bonsoir** (Good evening): Used once the sun goes down or the workday ends, typically from around 6:00 PM onward. 
*   **Salut** (Hi / Bye): An informal greeting used among friends, family, or peers of a similar age. It can be used at any time of day, but never with a stranger or someone in a position of authority.

Let's look at the pronunciation. You learned in Chapter 1 that the French 'R' is a guttural sound produced in the back of the throat. In *bonjour*, that 'R' comes right at the end. 

Also, recall the concept of the **E caduc** (the dropped E). If a word ending in a silent 'e' is followed by a word starting with a consonant, that 'e' is often dropped in spoken French. This happens frequently when we add a person's name to a greeting:
*   *Bonjour Étienne* becomes **Bonjour Étienne** (no change, because *bonjour* ends in an 'R').
*   *Bonjour Marie* often becomes **Bonj' Marie** in rapid, spoken French. 

### The Formal and Informal "You"

When you greet someone in English, you use the word "you." It doesn't matter if you are speaking to your best friend, your boss, or a group of five people. "You" does it all. 

In French, "you" is split into two completely different words based on formality and number. This distinction is the cornerstone of French social interaction.

*   **Tu**: This is the informal "you." It is singular, used for one person. You use *tu* when speaking to a friend, a family member, a classmate, a child, or a pet. Using *tu* implies closeness and familiarity.
*   **Vous**: This is the formal "you" and the plural "you." You use *vous* when speaking to a stranger, an elder, a boss, or anyone in a position of authority. It shows respect and distance. Crucially, you also use *vous* anytime you are speaking to more than one person, regardless of how close you are to them. If you are speaking to two of your best friends, you use *vous*.

The act of transitioning from *vous* to *tu* with someone is a significant social milestone in France. It is called **tutoyer** (to use *tu*). You should never initiate this transition with a superior; it must be offered by them. 

### Asking "How are you?"

Once you have said *Bonjour*, the next step is to ask how the person is doing. Again, the phrasing changes based on formality.

1.  **Comment allez-vous ?** (How are you? - Formal/Plural): This uses the formal *vous*. You will use this with the baker, a hotel receptionist, or an older relative you don't know well.
2.  **Comment vas-tu ?** (How are you? - Informal): This uses the informal *tu*. You use this with friends and peers.
3.  **Ça va ?** (How's it going? - Informal): This is the most common, casual way to ask someone how they are. It bypasses the formal/informal divide slightly because it doesn't contain a pronoun, though it is still generally casual.

A typical, polite exchange with a stranger looks like this:
> **You:** Bonjour, comment allez-vous ?
> **Baker:** Je vais bien, merci. Et vous ? (I am doing well, thank you. And you?)
> **You:** Très bien, merci. (Very well, thank you.)

### Farewells

Just as you must greet someone properly, you must leave them properly. 

*   **Au revoir** (Goodbye): The standard, universal farewell. It works in any situation, formal or informal. Notice the **E caduc** in *revoir*? In spoken French, the 'e' in *revoir* is often dropped, making it sound like *r'voir*. 
*   **Salut** (Bye): As mentioned earlier, *salut* doubles as a casual "bye" among friends.
*   **Bonne journée** (Have a good day): Used in the morning or afternoon when parting ways, wishing the person a good rest of their day.
*   **Bonne soirée** (Have a good evening): Used in the evening or night when parting ways, wishing the person a good rest of their evening.
*   **À bientôt** (See you soon): A friendly way to say you hope to see the person again in the near future.

## The Subject Pronouns

To have a conversation that goes beyond "Hello" and "How are you?", you need to start forming sentences. In French, every sentence requires a subject (the person or thing doing the action) and a verb (the action itself). 

The words we use to replace the subject's name are called **subject pronouns**. In English, these are *I, you, he, she, it, we, they*. 

Here are the French subject pronouns. Notice that there is no direct translation for the English word "it". In French, all nouns are either masculine or feminine, so "it" is always represented by "he" (il) or "she" (elle).

### Singular Pronouns (One person)

*   **Je** (I): Used when talking about yourself. 
    *   *Pronunciation note:* If *je* is followed by a word starting with a consonant, it sounds like [ʒə]. But if it is followed by a word starting with a vowel or a silent 'h', it drops its 'e' and attaches to the next word. This is the **E caduc** in action. *Je suis* (I am) becomes **J'suis** [ʒɥi] or [ʃɥi] in rapid spoken French.
*   **Tu** (You - singular, informal): Used for one friend, family member, child, or peer.
*   **Il** (He / It): Used for a masculine singular person or a masculine singular noun. 
*   **Elle** (She / It): Used for a feminine singular person or a feminine singular noun.
*   **On** (One / We / They): This is a highly versatile pronoun. Literally, it translates to "one" (as in "One should always say bonjour"). However, in modern, everyday spoken French, **on** is almost exclusively used to mean "we" instead of the actual word for "we" (*nous*). It takes a singular verb, making it easier to use. 

### Plural Pronouns (More than one person)

*   **Nous** (We): The formal, grammatically correct way to say "we." It is used in writing and formal speech.
*   **Vous** (You - plural OR formal singular): As established, this is used for groups of people, or for a single person you are showing respect to.
*   **Ils** (They - masculine / mixed): Used for a group of people or things that includes at least one masculine noun. If there are 99 women and 1 man in a room, you refer to the group as *ils*.
*   **Elles** (They - feminine): Used strictly for a group of entirely feminine people or things. If even one man joins the group of 99 women, the pronoun switches from *elles* to *ils*.

## The Verb "Être" (To Be)

Now that we know our pronouns, we need a verb to pair with them. The most essential verb in any language is the verb "to be." In French, this verb is **être**. 

**Être** is an **irregular verb**. This is a grammatical term meaning the verb does not follow the standard, predictable patterns of most other verbs. You simply have to memorize its forms. 

### Conjugating Être in the Present Tense

When we change a verb to match a specific subject pronoun, we call this **conjugating** the verb. Here is the present tense conjugation of *être*. 

*   **Je suis** (I am)
*   **Tu es** (You are - informal singular)
*   **Il / Elle / On est** (He is / She is / One is [or We are])
*   **Nous sommes** (We are)
*   **Vous êtes** (You are - formal singular OR plural)
*   **Ils / Elles sont** (They are)

#### Pronunciation Pitfalls

The conjugation of *être* contains some of the trickiest sounds in the French language, many of which we previewed in Chapter 1. Let's break them down:

*   **Suis**: The 'ui' combination makes a [ɥi] sound. It sounds very similar to the English word "swee" but compressed. 
*   **Es and Est**: These two forms are pronounced exactly the same way. The 's' and the 't' at the ends are **not pronounced**. They both sound like [ɛ]. 
*   **Sommes**: The 'o' makes an [ɔ] sound (like in "pot"), and the double 'm' makes a standard [m] sound.
*   **Êtes**: This is where your memory of Chapter 1's pronunciation rules is vital. The circumflex accent (the little hat: **ê**) generally indicates that an 's' used to exist in the historical spelling of the word (the Old French word was *estes*). That 's' was dropped, but it left a trace: the 'e' became an [ɛ] sound. So, *êtes* is pronounced [ɛt]. You *do* pronounce the 't' here because it is followed by a silent 's'. Wait, no—let's clarify: the 't' is pronounced because it is at the end of the word and followed by nothing, but in *est*, the 't' is silent. Why? Because *est* is a single syllable word where the consonant is silent, whereas *êtes* forces the [ɛ] sound and pronounces the final 't' [ɛt]. (When followed by a vowel in the next word, the 't' in *êtes* will liaison, e.g., *vous êtes-un...* [vu.zɛ.tœ̃]).
*   **Sont**: The 'o' is the nasal [ɔ̃] sound you learned about in the Golden Rule of Nasal Vowels. The 't' is silent.

## Introducing Yourself

By combining your subject pronouns with the verb *être*, you can now introduce yourself and share basic personal information. 

### Stating Your Name

There are two primary ways to say your name in French. Both use the verb *être*.

1.  **Je suis [Name].** (I am [Name].) 
    *   Example: *Je suis Thomas.* 
    *   Example: *Je suis Chloé.* 
2.  **Je m'appelle [Name].** (My name is [Name]. / I call myself [Name].)
    *   *Note:* We will cover the grammar behind *m'appelle* (reflexive verbs) in a later chapter. For now, treat *Je m'appelle* as a single phrase meaning "My name is." It is incredibly common and often preferred over *Je suis* for introductions.

### Stating Nationality and Profession

Because you are using the verb *être* (I am), you can easily state your nationality or your profession. In French, you do not use an article (the word "a") before a profession. You simply say "I am professor."

*   **Je suis américain.** (I am American - masculine). 
*   **Je suis américaine.** (I am American - feminine). 
    *   *Note:* Notice the extra 'e' and the pronunciation shift in the feminine form. We will explore the rules of masculine and feminine adjectives in Chapter 8. For now, just know that if you are a woman, you often add an 'e' to the end of nationality words, and if the base word ends in a consonant, that consonant becomes pronounced. *Américain* [a.me.ʁi.kɛ̃] (nasal vowel) becomes *américaine* [a.me.ʁi.kɛn] (hard 'n' sound).

*   **Je suis étudiant.** (I am a student - masculine).
*   **Je suis étudiante.** (I am a student - feminine).
*   **Je suis professeur.** (I am a teacher/professor - masculine).
*   **Je suis journaliste.** (I am a journalist - masculine or feminine). 

### A Complete Introduction

Let's put it all together. Imagine you are at a networking event in Lyon. You approach someone, and they offer their hand for a handshake. 

> **Stranger:** Bonjour ! Je m'appelle Marc. Comment allez-vous ?
> **You:** Bonjour Marc. Je suis [Your Name], je vais bien, merci. 
> **Stranger:** Enchanté ! (Nice to meet you!)
> **You:** Enchanté ! Je suis américain(e) et je suis étudiant(e). 
> **Stranger:** Ah, très bien. Au revoir, à bientôt !
> **You:** Au revoir, bonne journée !

### The "Tu" vs. "Vous" Test

If Marc were a 19-year-old student at a university party, he might introduce himself differently:
> **Marc:** Salut ! Je m'appelle Marc. Ça va ?
> **You:** Salut Marc. Je suis [Your Name]. Ça va bien, et toi ? (and you - informal)

Notice the shift? *Bonjour* became *Salut*. *Comment allez-vous* became *Ça va*. And the most important shift: *Et vous ?* (And you - formal) became **Et toi ?** (And you - informal). 

## Key Takeaways

*   **Greetings depend on time and formality:** Use *Bonjour* (morning/day) and *Bonsoir* (evening) with strangers and in formal settings. Use *Salut* (hi/bye) only with friends and peers.
*   **Tu vs. Vous:** *Tu* is the singular informal "you" used for friends, family, and children. *Vous* is the plural "you" for groups, and the singular formal "you" for strangers, elders, and authority figures. 
*   **Subject Pronouns:** French uses *je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles*. There is no direct word for "it" (everything is masculine or feminine). *On* is commonly used in spoken French to mean "we".
*   **Être (To Be):** This highly irregular verb is conjugated as: *je suis, tu es, il/elle/on est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils/elles sont*.
*   **Pronunciation matters:** Remember that *es* and *est* sound like [ɛ], *êtes* sounds like [ɛt], and *sont* uses the nasal [ɔ̃] sound.
*   **Introductions:** You can introduce yourself using *Je suis [Name]* or *Je m'appelle [Name]*, and state your profession or nationality directly after the verb *être* without adding an extra word for "a".

modules.title: Nouns, Gender, and Articles
modules.content:
## The Nature of French Nouns

Imagine you are looking at an apple. In English, you refer to it as "an apple." If you look at a piece of cheese, you say "a piece of cheese." The words "a" and "an" function perfectly well on their own, and the objects themselves have no inherent gender. 

In French, however, every single object, concept, and person is classified as either **masculine** or **feminine**. An apple (*une pomme*) is feminine. A piece of cheese (*un fromage*) is masculine. This is known as **grammatical gender**. 

A **noun** is a word that represents a person, place, thing, or idea. When learning French nouns, you cannot simply learn the word itself; you must learn its gender at the exact same time. Treating a noun as masculine when it is feminine (or vice versa) is considered a grammatical error in French. While native speakers will still understand you, matching the gender correctly is essential for speaking accurate French.

### Why Does French Have Gender?
French evolved from Latin, which categorized all nouns into three genders: masculine, feminine, and neuter. As the language evolved into Old French and eventually modern French, the neuter gender was absorbed into the masculine. Today, French retains only masculine and feminine classifications. 

It is crucial to understand that grammatical gender usually has nothing to do with biology. A table (*une table*) is not biologically female, and a wall (*un mur*) is not biologically male. The gender is simply an inherent grammatical feature of the word, much like a vehicle might be classified as a car or a truck. 

## Determining Masculine and Feminine Gender

Because there are no biological clues for inanimate objects, how do you know if a noun is masculine or feminine? The most reliable method is to memorize the noun with its article (which we will cover in the next section) from the very beginning. However, French does have spelling patterns that can help you guess the gender of a noun with a fair degree of accuracy.

### Spelling Patterns for Masculine Nouns
Nouns ending in the following letters or suffixes tend to be **masculine**:

*   **-on**: *un garçon* (a boy), *un mouton* (a sheep)
*   **-eau** / **-eaux**: *un bateau* (a boat), *un chapeau* (a hat)
*   **-ment**: *un appartement* (an apartment), *un monument* (a monument)
*   **-ier**: *un dossier* (a file), *un cahier* (a notebook)
*   **-age**: *un fromage* (cheese), *un village* (a village) 
    *   *Note: There are a few exceptions to this rule, such as **une image** (an image) and **une plage** (a beach).*

### Spelling Patterns for Feminine Nouns
Nouns ending in the following letters or suffixes tend to be **feminine**:

*   **-e**: *une table* (a table), *une chaise* (a chair)
    *   *Note: While most nouns ending in -e are feminine, many masculine nouns also end in -e (like **un livre** - a book). The -e rule is a strong hint, but the other suffixes on this list are much more reliable.*
*   **-tion**: *une nation* (a nation), *une information* (a piece of information)
*   **-té**: *une cité* (a city), *une université* (a university)
*   **-ure**: *une nature* (nature), *une voiture* (a car)
*   **-ette**: *une baguette* (a baguette), *une fourchette* (a fork)

When you encounter a new noun, look at its ending. If it ends in *-tion* or *-eau*, you can make a highly educated guess about its gender before you even look it up in a dictionary.

## Indefinite Articles: Un, Une, Des

An **article** is a small word that goes in front of a noun to indicate whether it is specific or general, and to reveal its gender and number (whether it is singular or plural). 

**Indefinite articles** are used when you are talking about something unspecific or unspecified—the equivalent of "a," "an," or "some" in English. You use them when the exact identity of the noun doesn't matter, or when you are introducing it for the first time. 

French has three indefinite articles:

1.  **Un** is used for **masculine singular** nouns.
    *   *un livre* (a book)
    *   *un chien* (a dog)
2.  **Une** is used for **feminine singular** nouns.
    *   *une pomme* (an apple)
    *   *une fille* (a girl)
3.  **Des** is used for **plural** nouns of both genders.
    *   *des livres* (some books)
    *   *des pommes* (some apples)

Notice that English often drops the word "some" in plural sentences. If you say "I am eating apples," you mean "some apples." In French, you cannot drop the article. You must say *Je mange des pommes*. 

**A note on pronunciation:** In Chapter 1, we covered the **[ø]** sound found in the word *deux*, and the **[e]** sound found in *café*. The indefinite article *des* is pronounced using a different sound: the **[ɛ]** (like the *è* in *mère*). It is pronounced like the English word "day," but spoken sharply without the gliding "y" sound at the end. 

## Definite Articles: Le, La, L', Les

While indefinite articles point to unspecific things ("a book," "some apples"), **definite articles** point to specific things. They are the equivalent of "the" in English. You use them when referring to a particular noun, or a noun that has already been mentioned. 

Because French nouns have gender, the definite article must match the noun's gender and number. There are four definite articles:

1.  **Le** is used for **masculine singular** nouns.
    *   *le livre* (the book)
    *   *le chien* (the dog)
2.  **La** is used for **feminine singular** nouns.
    *   *la pomme* (the apple)
    *   *la fille* (the girl)
3.  **L'** is used for **singular nouns of either gender** that begin with a vowel or a silent 'h'. 
    *   *l'arbre* (the tree - masculine)
    *   *l'école* (the school - feminine)
4.  **Les** is used for **plural** nouns of both genders.
    *   *les livres* (the books)
    *   *les pommes* (the apples)

### The Elision Rule: L'
When a singular noun begins with a vowel, saying *le* or *la* directly before it creates a clunky, halting sound. To keep the language flowing smoothly, French uses a process called **elision**. 

Elision is when a vowel at the end of a short word is dropped and replaced by an apostrophe, attaching the remaining consonant to the next word. 

*   *Le* + *ami* (friend, masculine) becomes **l'ami**.
*   *La* + *amie* (friend, feminine) becomes **l'amie**.

As we established in Chapter 1, vowels in French are the letters A, E, I, O, U, and Y. If a singular noun starts with any of these letters, you must use **l'**. You also use **l'** if the noun begins with a silent 'h' (known as an *h muet*). 

*   *l'homme* (the man)
*   *l'hôpital* (the hospital)

**A crucial warning:** Elision hides the gender of the word. *L'ami* (masculine) and *l'amie* (feminine) sound exactly the same when spoken aloud. When you learn a vowel-starting noun, you must make a special effort to memorize its gender, because you won't be able to hear it from the article.

### Pronunciation of Definite Articles
*   **Le** and **La**: The 'e' in both of these words utilizes the neutral "E caduc" sound we discussed in Chapter 1. It is pronounced as a soft, relaxed [ə], somewhat like the "u" in the English word "cup" (but with rounded lips). 
*   **Les**: The plural article *les* ends in a silent 's'. However, as we will explore in the pluralization rules below, that silent 's' wakes up and links to the next word if it begins with a vowel. 

## Forming Plural Nouns

So far, we have looked at singular nouns. But what happens when you have more than one book, or multiple apples? 

In English, we usually make a noun plural by adding an "s" to the end (book → books, apple → apples). French does exactly the same thing for most nouns, but it has several additional rules for words that end in different letters.

### Standard Spelling Rules for Plurals

1.  **Most nouns:** Add an **-s**.
    *   *un chat* (a cat) → *des chats* (some cats)
    *   *une voiture* (a car) → *des voitures* (some cars)
2.  **Nouns ending in -s, -x, or -z:** Do not change. The word is already considered plural in writing. You only know it is plural from the article (*les* or *des*).
    *   *un pays* (a country) → *des pays* (some countries)
    *   *un prix* (a price) → *des prix* (some prices)
    *   *un nez* (a nose) → *des nez* (some noses)
3.  **Nouns ending in -eau, -eu, or -ou:** Add an **-x**.
    *   *un bateau* (a boat) → *des bateaux* (some boats)
    *   *un feu* (a fire) → *des feux* (some fires)
    *   *un bisou* (a kiss) → *des bisoux* (some kisses)
    *   *Note: There are a few exceptions to the -ou rule, such as **un trou** → **des trous** (holes), but the -x rule applies to the vast majority.*
4.  **Nouns ending in -al:** Drop the **-al** and add **-aux**.
    *   *un animal* (an animal) → *des animaux* (some animals)
    *   *un journal* (a newspaper) → *des journaux* (some newspapers)
5.  **Nouns ending in -ail:** Generally add an **-s**.
    *   *un détail* (a detail) → *des détails* (some details)

### The Plural "S" is Usually Silent
In Chapter 1, we learned that consonants at the end of French words are generally **not pronounced**. This applies to the plural "-s". 

*chat* (singular) and *chats* (plural) sound exactly the same. You rely entirely on the article to tell the listener whether you mean one cat or multiple cats. You say *un chat* [œ̃ ʃa] for one, and *des chats* [de ʃa] for multiple.

### The Linking Rule: Les Liaisons
There is one major exception to the silent plural "s". This happens through a phonetic process called **liaison** (linking). 

When a plural noun begins with a vowel or a silent 'h', the silent 's' on the end of *les* or *des* connects to the beginning of the noun. It changes from an "s" sound to a "z" sound.

*   *Les amis* (the friends) is pronounced **[le.z‿a.mi]**. 
*   *Des enfants* (some children) is pronounced **[de.z‿ɑ̃.fɑ̃]**. 

Notice how the nasal vowel [ɑ̃] in *enfants* remains intact, but the 's' on *des* suddenly becomes vocalized as a [z] to bridge the gap between the article and the vowel-starting noun. If you forget the liaison, native speakers might momentarily think you are talking about a singular noun.

## Putting It All Together in Context

Let’s look at how these rules interact in a realistic scenario. Imagine you are setting up a new apartment in France and you are describing the items in your living room to a friend over the phone. 

You want to mention a specific chair, some general books, a specific table, and some general animals (let's say cats) you just adopted. 

Here is how gender, articles, and pluralization dictate your word choices:

*   **The chair:** The word for chair is *chaise*. It ends in an **-e**, which strongly hints it is feminine. Therefore, the singular definite article is *la*. You say: **la chaise**. 
*   **Some books:** The word for book is *livre*. It is masculine. Because you are talking about "some" unspecified books, you use the indefinite plural article *des*. You add an -s to the noun. You say: **des livres**. 
*   **The table:** The word for table is *table*. It ends in an **-e**, making it feminine. You are talking about a specific table. You say: **la table**.
*   **Some cats:** The word for cat is *chat*. It is masculine. You are talking about multiple, unspecified cats. You use *des* and add an -s. You say: **des chats**. (Remember, the 's' on *chats* is silent, so it sounds identical to the singular *chat*).

Now, what if we swap out a few of those items for vowel-starting words?

*   Instead of "the chair," you want to say "the armchair." The word is *fauteuil* (masculine). No vowel at the start, so it is simply **le fauteuil**.
*   Instead of "some books," you want to say "some newspapers." The word is *journal*. It ends in **-al**, so the plural is *journaux*. You say: **des journaux**.
*   Instead of "the table," you want to say "the TV." The word is *télé*. It ends in an **-é** (which we covered in Chapter 1) and is feminine. No vowel at the start, so it is **la télé**.
*   Instead of "some cats," you want to say "some friends." The word is *ami* (masculine) or *amie* (feminine). Both start with a vowel. You use *des*, and because of liaison, the 's' is pronounced like a 'z'. You say: **des amis** [de.z‿a.mi].

## Key Takeaways

*   **Grammatical Gender:** Every French noun is either masculine or feminine. This is a grammatical feature, usually unrelated to biology. You must memorize a noun's gender alongside its meaning.
*   **Spelling Clues:** Noun endings can help you guess gender. Common masculine endings include *-on*, *-eau*, *-ment*, and *-age*. Common feminine endings include *-tion*, *-té*, *-ure*, and *-ette*.
*   **Indefinite Articles (Un, Une, Des):** Used for unspecified nouns ("a," "an," "some"). Use *un* for masculine singular, *une* for feminine singular, and *des* for all plurals.
*   **Definite Articles (Le, La, L', Les):** Used for specific nouns ("the"). Use *le* for masculine singular, *la* for feminine singular, *l'* for singular nouns starting with a vowel or silent 'h', and *les* for all plurals.
*   **Elision:** When *le* or *la* meets a vowel-starting noun, the vowel drops and is replaced by an apostrophe (*l'arbre*, *l'école*). This hides the gender of the noun when spoken.
*   **Pluralization:** Most nouns take an **-s**. Nouns ending in *-s, -x, or -z* do not change. Nouns ending in *-eau, -eu, or -ou* generally take an **-x**. Nouns ending in *-al* change to **-aux**.
*   **Liaison:** The plural **-s** on *les* and *des* is normally silent, but if the plural noun begins with a vowel, the **-s** links to the noun and is pronounced as a **[z]** sound.

modules.title: Numbers, Dates, and Time
modules.content:
## Counting from Zero to Twenty

Imagine you are standing at a bustling Parisian bakery. The baker gestures to the golden, flaky croissants behind the glass and asks how many you would like. To survive this very first transaction—and to handle dates, times, and prices in the French-speaking world—you need to master numbers. 

In Chapter 1, we established the pronunciation rules that make French sound distinctively French. Now, we put those sounds to work. 

Let’s start at the very beginning. Here are the numbers from zero to twenty. We will use the **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)** to guide your pronunciation, relying on the vowel and consonant sounds introduced in Chapter 1.

*   **0 - zéro** [zeʁo]
*   **1 - un** [œ̃] *(Note: This uses a nasal vowel sound, but because it ends in 'n', the 'n' is not pronounced as a separate consonant.)*
*   **2 - deux** [dø] *(Uses the 'EU' combination [ø] sound).*
*   **3 - trois** [tʁwa]
*   **4 - quatre** [katʁ]
*   **5 - cinq** [sɛ̃k]
*   **6 - six** [sis]
*   **7 - sept** [sɛt]
*   **8 - huit** [ɥit]
*   **9 - neuf** [nœf]
*   **10 - dix** [dis]
*   **11 - onze** [ɔ̃z]
*   **12 - douze** [duz]
*   **13 - treize** [tʁɛz]
*   **14 - quatorze** [katɔʁz]
*   **15 - quinze** [kɛ̃z]
*   **16 - seize** [sɛz]
*   **17 - dix-sept** [dis.sɛt]
*   **18 - dix-huit** [di.zɥit]
*   **19 - dix-neuf** [diz.nœf]
*   **20 - vingt** [vɛ̃]

Notice the pattern starting at 17. French doesn't have unique, standalone words for seventeen, eighteen, and nineteen like English does. Instead, it uses a "ten-plus" math system: *dix-sept* (ten-seven), *dix-huit* (ten-eight), and *dix-neuf* (ten-nine). Keep this mathematical approach in mind; it will become very helpful as the numbers get larger.

## The Vigesimal System: Counting to 100

English operates on a strict base-ten system. Once you hit twenty, you simply add "one" to get twenty-one. French, however, uses a hybrid system. For the numbers 20 to 60, French behaves exactly like English. But from 70 to 99, French shifts into a **vigesimal system**—a base-twenty mathematical structure.

### 20 to 69: The Familiar Pattern

From 20 to 60, you have unique words for the tens:

*   **20 - vingt** [vɛ̃]
*   **30 - trente** [tʁɑ̃t]
*   **40 - quarante** [kaʁɑ̃t]
*   **50 - cinquante** [sɛ̃kɑ̃t]
*   **60 - soixante** [swasɑ̃t]

To say 21, 31, 41, and so on, you combine the ten with the unit using the word **et** (and). 

*   21 - *vingt et un* (twenty and one)
*   31 - *trente et un* (thirty and one)
*   42 - *quarante-deux* (forty-two) — *Note: You only use "et" when the number ends in 1. For all other numbers, just use a hyphen.*

### 70 to 99: The Math Puzzle

This is where beginners often pause. Instead of creating a new word for seventy, French does math based on sixty. 

*   **70 - soixante-dix** (sixty-ten)
*   71 - *soixante et onze* (sixty and eleven)
*   72 - *soixante-douze* (sixty-twelve)
*   73 - *soixante-treize* (sixty-thirteen)
*   ...and so on up to 79 (*soixante-dix-neuf*).

At 80, the base-twenty system kicks in fully. 
*   **80 - quatre-vingts** (four-twenties). Notice the 's' at the end of *vingts*. This is because there are multiple twenties. However, if you add any number after 80, the 's' disappears. 
*   81 - *quatre-vingt-un* (four-twenty-one)
*   82 - *quatre-vingt-deux* (four-twenty-two)

For 90, you combine the 80 structure with the teens:
*   **90 - quatre-vingt-dix** (four-twenty-ten)
*   91 - *quatre-vingt-onze* (four-twenty-eleven)
*   92 - *quatre-vingt-douze* (four-twenty-twelve)

Finally:
*   **100 - cent** [sɑ̃]

*(A quick note on regional variation: In Belgium and Switzerland, they have entirely separate words for 70, 80, and 90—septante, octante/huitante, and nonante—which avoid this math entirely. However, the system taught above is standard French, used in France and most of the Francophone world. It is the system you will hear most frequently.)*

## Days of the Week

Now that you can count, let’s look at how to organize your time. 

In Chapter 3, we learned about noun gender (masculine and feminine). The days of the week in French are a bit unique: **they are all masculine**, but they do not take a capital letter. In French, days of the week and months of the year are always written in lowercase unless they appear at the very beginning of a sentence.

*   **lundi** - Monday
*   **mardi** - Tuesday
*   **mercredi** - Wednesday
*   **jeudi** - Thursday
*   **vendredi** - Friday
*   **samedi** - Saturday
*   **dimanche** - Sunday

Notice that the days of the week end in **-di**. This comes from the Latin word for "day." *Lundi* translates literally to "Moon-day" (la lune is the moon), much like English!

To say that something happens *on* a specific day, you do not need a preposition like "on." You simply use the day of the week.

*   *I work Monday.* -> **Je travaille lundi.** (Note: "Je travaille" is a verb phrase you will learn to construct in Module 5; for now, focus on how *lundi* stands alone without an "on" equivalent).

If you want to say that something happens habitually *on* Mondays (every week), you add the article **le** in front of the day. As a masculine word, it takes **le**.

*   *I work on Mondays (every Monday).* -> **Je travaille le lundi.**

## Months of the Year

Like the days of the week, the months of the year are all **masculine** and are written in **lowercase**. 

*   **janvier** - January
*   **février** - February
*   **mars** - March
*   **avril** - April
*   **mai** - May
*   **juin** - June
*   **juillet** - July
*   **août** - August *(Note the circumflex accent over the 'u'. This is a diacritical mark, as covered in Chapter 1. Here, it indicates that the 'u' is pronounced [u], like the "oo" in "food".)*
*   **septembre** - September
*   **octobre** - October
*   **novembre** - November
*   **décembre** - December

To say "in" a specific month, you use the preposition **en**. 

*   *in January* -> **en janvier**
*   *in August* -> **en août**

## Saying the Date

Telling the date in French requires assembling the pieces we’ve just learned, along with one new concept. 

In Chapter 3, we learned that nouns generally require an article (like *le*, *la*, or *les*). When stating the date, French uses the definite article **le**. 

The formula for saying the full date in French is:
**Le** + [number] + [month]

*   *July 14th* -> **le 14 juillet** *(Note: French dates are almost always written with the day before the month).*
*   *December 25th* -> **le 25 décembre**

To say "Today is...", you use the phrase **Nous sommes** (We are) or **C'est** (It is). 

*   *Today is May 5th.* -> **Nous sommes le 5 mai.** (Or: **C'est le 5 mai.**)
*   *Today is October 3rd.* -> **Nous sommes le 3 octobre.**

### The First of the Month

There is one major exception to the date formula. In English, we might say "May 1st." In French, you cannot say "le 1 mai." The first day of the month has its own special word: **premier**. 

*   *May 1st* -> **le premier mai**
*   *January 1st* -> **le premier janvier**

For every other day of the month, you simply use the cardinal number (deux, trois, quatre, etc.).

## Telling Time: Quelle heure est-il ?

Now that you know your numbers and your days, it’s time to learn how to tell time. 

To ask "What time is it?", you say:
**Quelle heure est-il ?** [kɛl.œʁ.ɛ.til]

To answer, you use the phrase **Il est** (It is) followed by the hour. 

*   *It is one o'clock.* -> **Il est une heure.** 
*   *It is two o'clock.* -> **Il est deux heures.**
*   *It is three o'clock.* -> **Il est trois heures.**

Notice that the word for "hour" is **heure** [œʁ], and it is feminine. Because it is feminine, "one o'clock" uses the feminine article **une** (*une heure*), while all other hours use the plural article **des** (though in modern spoken French, the 'des' is often dropped, leaving just the number: *Il est deux heures*).

### Minutes and the 24-Hour Clock

In France and most of the French-speaking world, the **24-hour clock** (often called "military time" in the United States) is standard for schedules, train times, movie showings, and business hours. 

*   1:00 PM -> **treize heures** (13 hours)
*   6:00 PM -> **dix-huit heures** (18 hours)
*   Midnight -> **minuit** [minɥi] (zero hours, or *zéro heure*)
*   Noon -> **midi** [midi] (twelve hours, or *douze heures*)

To add minutes past the hour, you simply state the hour, the word *heures*, and the number of minutes.

*   3:15 -> **Il est trois heures quinze.** (It is three hours fifteen.)
*   5:30 -> **Il est cinq heures trente.** (It is five hours thirty.)

### Half, Quarter, and To the Hour

While the 24-hour clock is used for official scheduling, everyday conversation often uses the 12-hour format with fractions, much like English does.

*   **et quart** (and a quarter) - Used for 15 minutes past the hour.
    *   *It is 3:15.* -> **Il est trois heures et quart.**
*   **et demie** (and a half) - Used for 30 minutes past the hour. Note that *demie* gets an 'e' at the end because *heure* is feminine.
    *   *It is 3:30.* -> **Il est trois heures et demie.**
*   **moins le quart** (minus the quarter) - Used for 15 minutes before the hour.
    *   *It is 2:45.* -> **Il est trois heures moins le quart.** (Literally: It is three hours minus the quarter.)

To ask "At what time?" an event takes place, you use **À quelle heure ?**

*   *At what time is the meeting?* -> **À quelle heure est la réunion ?**
*   *At 3:00.* -> **À trois heures.** (Notice the preposition **à** meaning "at", which contracts with the masculine *le* to become *au*, but stays as *à* before the feminine *heure*).

## Parts of the Day

To give context to your times, you need to know the basic parts of the day. 

*   **le matin** - the morning
*   **l'après-midi** - the afternoon
*   **le soir** - the evening
*   **la nuit** - the night

You can attach these directly to your time phrases to clarify whether you mean AM or PM when using the 12-hour clock.

*   *It is 8:00 in the morning.* -> **Il est huit heures du matin.** *(Note: "du" is a contraction of "de" + "le").*
*   *It is 3:00 in the afternoon.* -> **Il est trois heures de l'après-midi.**
*   *It is 8:00 in the evening.* -> **Il est huit heures du soir.**

### A Cultural Note on "Bonsoir"

In Chapter 2, we learned the greeting **Bonjour** (Good morning/Good day). Once the afternoon transitions into the evening, the French switch to **Bonsoir** (Good evening). 

But when exactly does the *matin* become the *après-midi*, and when does the *après-midi* become the *soir*? 

Generally, the afternoon (*l'après-midi*) begins at noon (12:00 PM) and lasts until around 6:00 PM. At 6:00 PM, it becomes the evening (*le soir*), which lasts until bedtime or midnight, when it becomes *la nuit*. 

If you are greeting someone, a safe rule of thumb is to switch from *Bonjour* to *Bonsoir* around 6:00 PM. If you are leaving a dinner party at 10:00 PM, you would say **Bonne nuit** (Good night), as *nuit* implies the time when people are sleeping.

## Key Takeaways

*   French numbers 0–16 have unique words. From 17–19, French uses a "ten-plus" math system (*dix-sept*, *dix-huit*, *dix-neuf*).
*   From 70 to 99, standard French uses a base-twenty (vigesimal) math system. 70 is *soixante-dix* (sixty-ten), 80 is *quatre-vingts* (four-twenties), and 90 is *quatre-vingt-dix* (four-twenty-ten).
*   Days of the week (*lundi, mardi, mercredi...*) and months of the year (*janvier, février, mars...*) are all masculine and are written in lowercase.
*   To say the date, use the formula: **Le** + [number] + [month]. The exception is the first of the month, which uses **premier** (e.g., *le premier mai*).
*   To ask the time, say **Quelle heure est-il ?** To answer, use **Il est** + [number] + **heures**.
*   France uses the 24-hour clock for official schedules, but conversational time uses fractions like **et quart** (quarter past), **et demie** (half past), and **moins le quart** (quarter to).
*   The parts of the day are **le matin** (morning), **l'après-midi** (afternoon), **le soir** (evening), and **la nuit** (night). Switch from saying *Bonjour* to *Bonsoir* around 6:00 PM.

modules.title: Regular Verbs in the Present Tense
modules.content:
## The Engine of the Language

Imagine you are sitting at a sunny Parisian café. The waiter approaches, and you want to say, "I speak a little French," or perhaps ask, "Do you sell croissants?" To do this, you need verbs. 

In French, verbs are the engine of a sentence. They communicate the action or the state of being. But unlike in English, where we mostly rely on words like "I," "you," or "we" to tell us who is doing the action (e.g., "I speak" vs. "you speak"), French changes the spelling—and often the pronunciation—of the verb itself depending on who is acting. 

In Chapter 2, you met the **subject pronouns** (*je, tu, il, elle, nous, vous, ils, elles*). Now, it is time to attach verbs to them.

### What is an Infinitive?

Before we can change a verb to match a subject, we have to look at its base form. The base form of a verb is called the **infinitive**. 

Think of the infinitive as the verb's natural state, before it has been put to work in a sentence. In English, infinitives usually include the word "to":
*   to speak
*   to finish
*   to sell

In French, there is no separate word for "to." Instead, the infinitive is a single word that always ends in one of three combinations of letters: **-er**, **-ir**, or **-re**. 

Here are the three verbs we will use as our models for this chapter:
*   **Parler** (to speak) — ends in **-er**
*   **Finir** (to finish) — ends in **-ir**
*   **Vendre** (to sell) — ends in **-re**

These three endings divide all French verbs into three main families or **groups**. 

## The Concept of Conjugation

When we take an infinitive and change its ending to match a specific subject pronoun, we are **conjugating** the verb. 

Think of a verb as a train. The infinitive ending (like **-er**) is the coupling that connects the train cars together. When we conjugate the verb, we remove that coupling—this is called finding the **stem** or **root**. Then, we attach a new set of letters—the **ending**—that matches the subject.

Let's break that down:
1.  Drop the infinitive ending (-er, -ir, or -re).
2.  What remains is the stem.
3.  Add the specific ending for the subject pronoun you want to use.

While French has hundreds of verbs, the vast majority of them are **regular**. This means they follow predictable, mathematical rules. Once you learn the pattern for one group, you can conjugate almost any verb in that family. (We will tackle the rebellious "irregular" verbs in Chapter 6).

Let’s look at each group individually.

## Group 1: Regular -er Verbs

The **-er** verbs are the largest family in French, making up roughly 90% of all verbs in the language. If you learn this pattern, you have unlocked a massive portion of French vocabulary.

Our model verb is **parler** (to speak).

### Step 1: Find the stem
Take the infinitive **parler** and drop the **-er**. 
The stem is **parl-**.

### Step 2: Add the endings
Here is the set of endings for regular **-er** verbs. Notice how they correspond exactly to the subject pronouns you learned in Chapter 2.

*   **je** parl**e** (I speak)
*   **tu** parl**es** (you speak)
*   **il / elle / on** parl**e** (he / she / one speaks)
*   **nous** parl**ons** (we speak)
*   **vous** parl**ez** (you speak - plural or formal)
*   **ils / elles** parl**ent** (they speak)

### Pronunciation Rules for -er Verbs

French spelling can look intimidating, but the pronunciation of regular **-er** verbs is wonderfully consistent. Remembering the rules from Chapter 1 on pronunciation will help immensely here:

1.  **The silent endings:** The *je*, *tu*, *il/elle/on*, and *ils/elles* forms all end in a silent letter. Whether it is an 'e', 'es', or 'ent', **you do not pronounce it**. 
    *   *je parle*, *tu parles*, and *ils parlent* are all pronounced exactly the same way: **[paʁl]**.
2.  **The [e] sound:** Because the consonant before the final silent 'e' (the letter 'l' in *parle*) is pronounced, your voice naturally slides into the **[e]** sound (like the 'a' in "gate") for the *je, tu,* and *il* forms. 
3.  **The É and È sounds:** The *vous* form ends in **-ez**, which produces the pure **[e]** sound. The *nous* form ends in **-ons**. The 'o' in 'ons' produces an **[ɔ̃]** nasal vowel sound, making *parlons* sound like **[paʁlɔ̃]**.

### Other common -er verbs
Because **-er** verbs are regular, you can swap out the stem and apply the exact same endings:
*   **Manger** (to eat): *je mange, tu manges, il mange, nous mangeons, vous mangez, ils mangent*
*   **Travailler** (to work): *je travaille, tu travailles, il travaille, nous travaillons, vous travaillez, ils travaillent*
*   **Habiter** (to live): *j'habite, tu habites, il habite, nous habitons, vous habitez, ils habitent*

*(Note: Remember the E caduc rule from Chapter 1! If the verb starts with a vowel or a silent 'h', like "habiter", "je" becomes "j'" to flow smoothly: j'habite).*

## Group 2: Regular -ir Verbs

The second group of regular verbs ends in **-ir**. While this group is smaller than the **-er** group, it contains many highly useful verbs. 

Our model verb is **finir** (to finish).

### Step 1: Find the stem
Take the infinitive **finir** and drop the **-ir**.
The stem is **fin-**.

### Step 2: Add the endings
Regular **-ir** verbs have a distinct signature. Notice how the singular pronouns (*je, tu, il/elle/on*) all get an **-is** ending, and the plural pronouns get **-issons, -issez, -issent**. The repeating 'ss' is the hallmark of this group.

*   **je** fin**is** (I finish)
*   **tu** fin**is** (you finish)
*   **il / elle / on** fin**it** (he / she / one finishes)
*   **nous** fin**issons** (we finish)
*   **vous** fin**issez** (you finish)
*   **ils / elles** fin**issent** (they finish)

### Pronunciation Rules for -ir Verbs

Like the **-er** verbs, the pronunciation for this group is highly regular:

1.  **The silent 'ent':** Just as before, the **-ent** ending on the *ils/elles* form is entirely silent. 
2.  **The [i] sound:** The *je, tu,* and *il/elle* forms all end in an 's' or 't' right after an 'i'. This gives them a crisp **[i]** sound (like the 'ee' in "feet"). *Je finis* and *ils finissent* sound identical: **[fini]**.
3.  **The nasal vowel:** The *nous* form (*finissons*) and the *ils/elles* form (*finissent*) contain the **[i]** sound followed by the **[ɔ̃]** nasal vowel. *Vous finissez* uses the **[e]** sound for the **-ez** ending.

### Other common -ir verbs
*   **Choisir** (to choose): *je choisis, tu choisis, il choisit, nous choisissons, vous choisissez, ils choisissent*
*   **Réussir** (to succeed): *je réussis, tu réussis, il réussit, nous réussissons, vous réussissez, ils réussissent*
*   **Grandir** (to grow): *je grandis, tu grandis, il grandit, nous grandissons, vous grandissez, ils grandissent*

## Group 3: Regular -re Verbs

The third and smallest group of regular verbs ends in **-re**. 

Our model verb is **vendre** (to sell).

### Step 1: Find the stem
Take the infinitive **vendre** and drop the **-re**.
The stem is **vend-**.

### Step 2: Add the endings
The **-re** group is unique because it does not have an ending for the *je, tu, il/elle/on*, and *ils/elles* forms. You simply use the stem, adding an 's' for *je, tu,* and *ils/elles*, and nothing for *il/elle/on*.

*   **je** vend**s** (I sell)
*   **tu** vend**s** (you sell)
*   **il / elle / on** vend (he / she / one sells)
*   **nous** vend**ons** (we sell)
*   **vous** vend**ez** (you sell)
*   **ils / elles** vend**ent** (they sell)

### Pronunciation Rules for -re Verbs

1.  **The silent consonants:** The 's' at the end of *je vends* and *tu vends* is silent. The 'd' at the end of *il vend* is silent. The 'ent' at the end of *ils vendent* is silent. Therefore, *je vends, tu vends, il vend,* and *ils vendent* are all pronounced exactly the same: **[vɑ̃d]**.
2.  **The nasal vowel:** The *nous* form ends in **-ons**, giving us the **[ɔ̃]** nasal vowel: **[vɑ̃dɔ̃]**.
3.  **The [e] sound:** The *vous* form ends in **-ez**, giving us the **[e]** sound: **[vɑ̃de]**.

### Other common -re verbs
*   **Attendre** (to wait): *j'attends, tu attends, il attend, nous attendons, vous attendez, ils attendent*
*   **Perdre** (to lose): *je perds, tu perds, il perd, nous perdons, vous perdez, ils perdent*
*   **Répondre** (to answer): *je réponds, tu réponds, il répond, nous répondons, vous répondez, ils répondent*

## Building Simple Affirmative Sentences

Now that you have your engines (verbs) and your drivers (subject pronouns), you are ready to build complete, affirmative sentences. 

The basic word order in French is the same as in English: **Subject + Verb + Object**. 

Because the conjugated verb already tells us who is doing the action, the subject pronoun is sometimes dropped in casual speech (just like we say "Going to the store" instead of "I am going to the store" in English). However, for now, it is best to include the subject pronoun to practice your conjugations.

Let's look at a realistic scenario. You are at a market in Lyon, interacting with a vendor and a friend. 

**Scenario: At the Market**

*   **You** want to tell the vendor what you are looking for. You use the verb *chercher* (to look for - an **-er** verb).
    *   *Je cherche du fromage.* (I am looking for some cheese.)
*   **The vendor** tells you about his products. He uses the verb *vendre* (to sell - a **-re** verb).
    *   *Je vends du fromage de chèvre.* (I sell goat cheese.)
*   **Your friend** asks if you both can wait for a moment. She uses the verb *attendre* (to wait - a **-re** verb).
    *   *Nous attendons une minute.* (We are waiting a minute.)
*   **The vendor** tells you both that he is closing soon. He uses the verb *finir* (to finish - an **-ir** verb).
    *   *Je finis à midi.* (I finish at noon.)
*   **You** reply to your friend using the verb *choisir* (to choose - an **-ir** verb).
    *   *Tu choisis le fromage.* (You choose the cheese.)
*   **The vendor** tells you what the people in the market generally do. He uses the verb *acheter* (to buy - an **-er** verb).
    *   *Ils achètent le pain ici.* (They buy the bread here.)

Notice how in every single sentence, the verb changes its ending to match the subject. Once you know the stem of the verb and the group it belongs to, you can plug it into any sentence.

### A Note on Context
Remember from Chapter 2 that *tu* is used for friends, family, and peers, while *vous* is used for formal situations (like talking to a market vendor you don't know) or plural groups. If you were speaking formally to the cheese vendor, you would say:
*   *Vous vendez du fromage de chèvre ?* (Do you sell goat cheese?)

## Practice Exercise: Identify and Conjugate

Read the following short paragraph. Notice the infinitives in parentheses. Try to identify which group they belong to (-er, -ir, or -re) based on their endings, then mentally conjugate them before reading the translation below.

> "Je (travailler) dans un bureau. Tu (choisir) les projets. Il (attendre) le bus. Nous (parler) français. Vous (réussir) ! Ils (vendre) des ordinateurs."

**Conjugation Breakdown:**
1.  **Travailler** (-er) -> *je travaille* (I work)
2.  **Choisir** (-ir) -> *tu choisis* (you choose)
3.  **Attendre** (-re) -> *il attend* (he waits)
4.  **Parler** (-er) -> *nous parlons* (we speak)
5.  **Réussir** (-ir) -> *vous réussissez* (you succeed)
6.  **Vendre** (-re) -> *ils vendent* (they sell)

By simply looking at the last two letters of the infinitive, you immediately know which set of rules to apply. 

## Key Takeaways

*   The **infinitive** is the base form of a verb before it is conjugated. In French, infinitives always end in **-er**, **-ir**, or **-re**.
*   **Conjugating** a verb means changing its ending to match a subject pronoun (je, tu, il/elle/on, nous, vous, ils/elles).
*   To conjugate a regular verb, drop the infinitive ending to find the **stem**, then add the specific endings for that verb group.
*   **-er verbs** (like *parler*) are the most common. Their endings are: *-e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent*.
*   **-ir verbs** (like *finir*) have a distinctive double 's' in their plural endings: *-is, -is, -it, -issons, -issez, -issent*.
*   **-re verbs** (like *vendre*) have minimal endings: *-s, -s, -, -ons, -ez, -ent*.
*   Pronunciation is highly consistent: the **-ent** ending on the *ils/elles* form is always silent. The *vous* form ending in **-ez** always sounds like **[e]**.
*   Basic French sentence structure follows the **Subject + Verb + Object** order, just like in English.

modules.title: Essential Irregular Verbs
modules.content:
## Meet the Heavyweights

Imagine you are sitting in a Parisian café, listening to the conversations around you. Within minutes, you will hear the verbs *avoir* (to have), *aller* (to go), and *faire* (to do/make). These three verbs are the absolute heavyweights of the French language. 

In the previous chapter, you learned how regular verbs work. You saw that verbs ending in *-er* follow a predictable pattern: you drop the *-er* and add specific endings depending on the subject pronoun (*je, tu, il/elle, nous, vous, ils/elles*). 

Unfortunately, the most frequently used verbs in French refuse to follow these rules. They are **irregular verbs**, meaning their stems change and their endings do not conform to the standard patterns you just learned. Because they are so common, you cannot avoid them—you simply have to memorize them. 

The good news is that once you master these three, you unlock the ability to express age, talk about your plans, and describe your hobbies and daily activities. Let’s start with the most essential verb in the French language.

## The Verb "Avoir" (To Have)

The verb **avoir** means "to have." 

In English, we use the verb "to be" to describe states of being, such as age or physical sensations. French does things differently. In French, *avoir* is a multipurpose verb that handles not only possession but also age and many physical states.

Here is its conjugation in the present tense, along with the **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)** pronunciation to help you. 

*   **j'ai** [ʒe] — I have
*   **tu as** [ty ɑ] — you have
*   **il/elle a** [il/ɛl ɑ] — he/she has
*   **nous avons** [nu za vɔ̃] — we have
*   **vous avez** [vu za ve] — you have (formal/plural)
*   **ils/elles ont** [il/ɛl zɔ̃] — they have

### Pronunciation Notes for Avoir
*   **The silent "h" and liaisons:** The pronoun *je* becomes **j'** before *avoir* because *avoir* begins with a vowel. This prevents the awkward clash of two vowel sounds. 
*   **Liaison with nous, vous, and ils/elles:** Notice the [z] in the IPA for *nous avons*, *vous avez*, and *ils/elles ont*. When a pronoun ending in a silent "s" is followed by a verb starting with a vowel, you pronounce the "s" as a [z] to create a smooth transition. This is a **liaison**.
*   **Nasal vowels:** The "on" in *nous avons* and *ils/elles ont* uses the nasal vowel [ɔ̃] that you practiced in the Foundations chapter. Remember the Golden Rule of Nasal Vowels: the "n" is not pronounced as a separate consonant; it simply signals that the preceding vowel is nasalized.

### Expressing Age with Avoir
The most important difference between French and English regarding *avoir* is how we express age. In English, we say "I *am* twenty years old." In French, you say "I *have* twenty years."

*   *J'ai vingt ans.* (I am twenty years old. / Literally: I have twenty years.)
*   *Il a trente-cinq ans.* (He is thirty-five years old.)
*   *Vous avez quel âge ?* (How old are you? / Literally: You have what age?)

Notice that the word for "year" (*an*) is plural (*ans*) because the age is greater than one. 

### Common Expressions with Avoir
French uses *avoir* to describe physical states that English describes with "to be." You will encounter these constantly:

*   **Avoir faim** — to be hungry (Literally: to have hunger)
    *   *J'ai faim.* (I am hungry.)
*   **Avoir soif** — to be thirsty 
    *   *Tu as soif.* (You are thirsty.)
*   **Avoir besoin de** — to need (Literally: to have need of)
    *   *Nous avons besoin de café.* (We need coffee.)

When using **avoir besoin de**, remember that *de* is a preposition. If you need a specific thing, you just attach it right after:
*   *J'ai besoin de* **un stylo**. (I need a pen.)
*   *Elle a besoin de* **sommeil**. (She needs sleep.)

## The Verb "Aller" (To Go)

The verb **aller** means "to go." It is a uniquely irregular verb. Not only does its stem change radically, but its endings actually resemble those of regular *-er* verbs in the plural forms. However, the singular forms are completely unique.

Here is its present tense conjugation:

*   **je vais** [ʒə vɛ] — I go
*   **tu vas** [ty va] — you go
*   **il/elle va** [il/ɛl va] — he/she goes
*   **nous allons** [nu za lɔ̃] — we go
*   **vous allez** [vu za le] — you go (formal/plural)
*   **ils/elles vont** [il/ɛl vɔ̃] — they go

### Pronunciation Notes for Aller
*   **The E caduc:** In *je vais* [ʒə vɛ], the "e" in *je* is pronounced as a soft [ə] (the *e caduc*). However, in rapid, everyday speech, French speakers frequently drop this *e caduc*, turning *je vais* into *j'vais* [ʒvɛ]. Both are correct, but you will hear the dropped version constantly in France.
*   **Liaisons:** Just like with *avoir*, you must make a liaison with the plural pronouns: *nous allons* [nu za lɔ̃] and *vous allez* [vu za le]. 
*   **Nasal vowels:** The "ons" ending in *nous allons* and *ils/elles vont* uses the [ɔ̃] nasal vowel. 

### Using Aller to Express Movement
*Aller* is your go-to verb for expressing where you are heading. To say where you are going, you pair the verb with the preposition **à** (to/at). 

*   *Je vais à la banque.* (I am going to the bank.)
*   *Nous allons au cinéma.* (We are going to the movie theater.)

*Note on prepositions:* You might notice that "to the" changed from *à la* to *au* in the second example. This is because of the gender of the nouns. *Banque* is feminine, so it uses *à la*. *Cinéma* is masculine, so *à* + *le* contracts into *au*. We will explore prepositions of location and movement in greater depth in Chapter 11, but for now, just know that *aller* pairs with *à* to mean "going to."

## The Verb "Faire" (To Do / To Make)

The verb **faire** is incredibly versatile. It translates to both "to do" and "to make," and it is the engine behind dozens of everyday activities, from playing sports to doing chores to crafting objects.

Here is its present tense conjugation:

*   **je fais** [ʒə fɛ] — I do/make
*   **tu fais** [ty fɛ] — you do/make
*   **il/elle fait** [il/ɛl fɛ] — he/she does/makes
*   **nous faisons** [nu fə zɔ̃] — we do/make
*   **vous faites** [vu fɛt] — you do/make (formal/plural)
*   **ils/elles font** [il/ɛl fɔ̃] — they do/make

### Pronunciation Notes for Faire
*   **The "ai" and "ai" combination:** As you learned in the Foundations chapter, the combination "ai" generally produces the [ɛ] sound (like the "e" in "bed"). You can hear this clearly in *je fais, tu fais, il/elle fait*, and *vous faites*.
*   **The surprise in "nous faisons":** This is a major exception. In almost every other verb, the "ons" ending is pronounced [ɔ̃] (like in *nous allons*). But in *nous faisons*, the "ai" is pronounced as a soft [ə] (the *e caduc* sound): [nu fə zɔ̃]. Be careful not to say [nu fe zɔ̃]!
*   **The irregular "vous" form:** Most verbs have a "vous" form that ends in *-ez*, pronounced [e]. *Faire* breaks this rule entirely. *Vous faites* is pronounced [vu fɛt] with a hard [t] sound at the end. 
*   **Nasal vowels:** The *ils/elles font* form uses the [ɔ̃] nasal vowel.

### Common Expressions with Faire
*Faire* is used to construct many common activities. Here are the most important ones for a beginner to know:

**Playing Sports**
